summary:
The Kingdom of Commagene (163–72 BC, then 38 BC–72 AD) was a small but culturally remarkable Hellenistic kingdom in southeastern Anatolia, founded when Ptolemaeus of Commagene declared independence from the Seleucid Empire. Its most famous ruler, Antiochus I (69–36 BC), created one of antiquity's most extraordinary monuments at Mount Nemrut: colossal stone thrones with giant sculpted heads of gods and the king himself, inscribed with proclamations asserting his divine ancestry from both the Persian Achaemenids and Alexander the Great.

Commagene occupied a strategic position between Rome, Parthia, and the Hellenistic successor kingdoms, and its rulers skillfully navigated these competing powers through diplomatic marriages and calculated neutrality. Antiochus I promoted a syncretic religion blending Greek and Persian divine figures — Zeus-Oromasdes, Apollo-Mithras, and others — reflecting the kingdom's position as a cultural crossroads. The kingdom was briefly annexed by Rome then restored before being permanently incorporated as the Roman province of Syria Commagene in 72 AD.
